exemplar.title: How does Sappho utilise natural imagery in order to depict the tragic effect of desire on individuality?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does Sappho utilise natural imagery in order to depict the tragic effect of desire on individuality?

subject.title: English Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Literature focuses exclusively on the study of literature, developing students' appreciation and critical analysis of literary works from various cultures, periods, and genres. Students examine poetry, prose, and drama through close reading, comparative analysis, and exploration of literary techniques and themes. The course cultivates sophisticated interpretive skills and deep understanding of how literature reflects and shapes human experience across different cultural contexts.

strengths: Demonstrates sound engagement with multiple Sappho fragments, showing sustained central argument.
Integrates quotations from primary texts with appropriate line references and a bibliography.
weaknesses: Misinterpretation of key allusions (e.g., treating Achillesâ apple as biblical) reveals gaps in contextual understanding.
Limited use of secondary sources weakens technical claims about metrics and historical context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate shows more than minimal knowledgeâengaging with several fragments and sustaining an argumentâbut recurring conceptual inaccuracies and superficial deployment of references prevent this from reaching Band 3. To align with the overall mark of 10, Knowledge is scaled to 2/5, reflecting adequate but flawed understanding.

strengths: Offers generally appropriate analysis of imagery and metaphor (e.g., juxtaposition of moon and stars).
Includes some insightful close reading (enjambment in âYouth and Ageâ).
weaknesses: Frequent lapses into paraphrase and repetition limit depth of evaluation.
Key claims (e.g., moon as âall-powerful,â metrics symbolizing desireâs weakness) lack textual or scholarly support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis moves beyond mere description but is not consistently sustained or insightful, with unsupported assertions and repetitive points. This aligns with lower Band 3 performance. Scaled to 2/5 to reflect generally appropriate but uneven analysis under the constraint of a 10-point total.

strengths: Essay is well organized around clear thematic sections with effective topic sentences.
Supporting examples are mostly well integrated, and transitions guide the reader logically.
weaknesses: Occasional digressions and abrupt thematic shifts interrupt cohesion.
Some long quotations lack full commentary, slightly weakening development.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The structure is coherent and the line of inquiry is well developed, meriting a high mark. Given the overall limit of 10 marks, Focus, Organization and Development receives 4/5 as the strongest area in the submission.

strengths: Vocabulary is varied and register is largely academic and appropriate.
Sentences are generally readable and convey complex ideas.
weaknesses: Multiple typos and awkward constructions (e.g., âportraval,â run-on sentences) detract from overall clarity.
Inconsistent bibliography formatting and occasional imprecise phrasing reveal lapses in proofreading.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Language is clear at times but marred by frequent errors in grammar, spelling and style. This performance falls in lower Band 3, scaled to 2/5 to satisfy the required total of 10 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Quotations are generally woven into sentences (âSapphoâs âtongue breaking downâ...â) rather than merely appended, and analytical comments usually follow directly after each example. Minor lapsesâoccasional floating quotations or long block citations without full commentaryâprevent the integration from being flawless, but it remains âmostly well integrated,â meriting Band 4.

The student demonstrates a sound grasp of Sapphoâs major themesâdesire, mortality and identityâand can relate them to several fragments with a sustained central argument. However, a number of conceptual inaccuracies (e.g., treating Achillesâ apple as a biblical rather than mythological allusion) reveal gaps in contextual understanding, and the explanation of metrics in Fragment 31 is asserted rather than evidenced. These errors prevent the work from reaching the âgood/persuasiveâ threshold, yet the breadth of textual familiarity and generally coherent interpretation clearly exceed the âsome knowledgeâ level. Hence, Band 3 is most fitting.

The essay follows a clear line of inquiry set out in the introduction and develops it through three thematic sections (biblical/natural, celestial, animalistic imagery) before a concise conclusion. Topic sentences guide the reader, transitions are logical, and ideas are mostly cohesive, though some digressions and repetition occur. The structure therefore meets the âwell organised and mostly cohesiveâ description of Band 4.

Quotations from Fragments 31, 34, 48, 58, 96, 105a and the poem âYouth and Ageâ are provided with line references and a bibliography, showing regular engagement with primary material. Most citations are relevant to the points being made, but some are deployed superficially or attached to misinterpretations, which weakens their effectiveness. Because the references âmostlyâ support ideas rather than doing so âeffectivelyâ or âpersuasivelyâ throughout, Band 3 (generally relevant and mostly supportive) is appropriate.

The register is academic and largely consistent, using appropriate critical terminology and avoiding colloquialisms. Nevertheless, occasional imprecise phrasing (âSappho effortlessly portraysâ) and over-statement (âonly once she dies she will regain her innocenceâ) detract from stylistic effectiveness. Thus the work is âmostly appropriateâ rather than consistently polished, corresponding to Band 3.

Language is generally clear and readable; vocabulary is varied (âliminalityâ, âgynocentricâ, âvenerationâ) and sentences are mostly well-constructed. However, syntactic awkwardness, run-on sentences, and several spelling/typing errors (âportravalâ, ânaturalistic entitiesâ) appear throughout. These issues indicate âadequateâ rather than âgoodâ accuracy, placing the work in Band 3.

The commentary moves beyond description by analysing imagery (fire, moon, stars, raven), metaphor and juxtaposition, and by linking these features to the thesis about desire eroding individuality. Nevertheless, analysis often drifts into paraphrase, repeats similar points, or draws tenuous conclusions (e.g., âmoon as objective source of light therefore all-powerfulâ). There are glimpses of evaluation but they are not sustained or consistently insightful. Overall it aligns with Band 3: âgenerally appropriate analysis and evaluation.â

annotations.comment: The assertion that the moon is âall-powerfulâ lacks textual support. Consider examining how Sapphoâs passive imagery actually undercuts rather than empowers celestial bodies.
When discussing the âstarsâ fading, clarify how this visual effect parallels Sapphoâs diminishing self-esteem, and provide a deeper interpretation of that symbolism.
The term ânaturalistic entitiesâ is vague here. Specify which natural images you mean and explain their particular literary function.
Your topic sentence clearly signals the shift to celestial imagery and sets up the paragraphâs argument effectively.
The claim that the moon is âinherently gynocentricâ assumes specialized knowledge. Define this concept to ensure readers follow your argument.
You discuss animalistic imagery in isolation here; could you tie it back to earlier natural imagery discussions to reinforce cohesion across sections?

The introduction effectively identifies key themes and imagery categories, providing a clear framework for the argument. However, the thesis sentence is quite lengthy and could be condensed to improve clarity and focus.
The reading of âAchillesâ appleâ as a biblical allusion conflates mythological and biblical traditions. Consider revisiting classical sources to accurately contextualize this reference.

The claim that Fragment 31âs broken metrics symbolize desireâs weakness is intriguing but unsupported. Incorporate metrical examples or scholarly references to substantiate this technical analysis.
Typographical error: âportravalâ should be âportrayal.â Consistent proofreading will enhance overall language accuracy and professionalism.
Transitions between discussions of biblical and natural imagery feel abrupt. Consider adding brief linking sentences to reinforce coherence between these thematic shifts.
The phrase âvoyeuristic observationâ appears promising but isnât developed. How does this perspective deepen your interpretation of desireâs impact on identity?
The integration of Fragment 31âs quotation is well done, as you weave it seamlessly into your own sentence structure and link it to your analysis.
The sentence âThe moon is continuously a powerful literary tool as it is merely a passive reflection of lightâ is awkward. Consider splitting it or rephrasing for clarity.
This paragraph contains a very long sentence starting with âThrough Sapphoâs other fragmentsâ¦â which hampers readability. Break it into two sentences.
Your analysis of the juxtaposition between âmoonâ and âstarsâ insightfully highlights Sapphoâs vulnerability.
The statement that the moon âlights all the spaceâ overgeneralizes the metaphor. Consider a more nuanced reading of how celestial imagery constrains the speaker.
Add specific line numbers for Fragment 34 (and others) when quoting. This will strengthen your textual grounding and allow easier cross-reference.
You ask âa lonely womanâ in Fragment 48 how isolation shapes identityâcould you explore how this theme connects to Sapphoâs communal context?
Your close reading of enjambment in âYouth and Ageâ reveals a sophisticated understanding of poetic form and its thematic resonance.
The claim that death is âthe only sourceâ of ending desire is an overstatement. Consider qualifying this or exploring moments where Sappho seeks emotional resolution elsewhere.
When quoting Fragment 58âs raven metaphor, include the full line to allow precise analysis of diction and imagery.
The phrase ânaturality of ageingâ is unclear. Revising it to ânatural progression of ageingâ will improve precision.
The conclusion succinctly synthesizes your key arguments and effectively reflects on the universal nature of desire.
Only one secondary source is cited in your bibliography. Incorporating additional scholarly works on metrics or historical context would strengthen your technical claims.
Bibliography format is incomplete: include publisher location and conform to a standard citation style consistently.
Consider integrating a critical source on Sapphoâs Greek metrics or cultural context to underpin your analysis of form and allusion.
